case_name: Radium removal from municipal groundwater for drinking water compliance
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for radium-contaminated groundwater intended for drinking water supply.

input_data.scenario_background: A small municipal utility relies on deep groundwater wells and has detected combined radium above drinking water standards. The raw water is otherwise relatively clear and microbiologically stable, so the utility wants a conventional and well-documented treatment approach rather than an advanced high-energy system. Operators can manage routine chemical feed or vessel-based treatment, but they prefer dependable processes with straightforward compliance monitoring.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should comply with drinking water requirements for combined radium while maintaining stable finished water quality for municipal distribution.
secondary_goals: use proven and established treatment technology
keep energy use moderate to low
maintain low to medium operational complexity
avoid unnecessary advanced treatment if simpler compliant options are available

engineering_constraints.residuals_management: radioactive residuals and brine should be minimized but managed through approved off-site disposal if needed
engineering_constraints.robustness_requirement: stable year-round operation with predictable compliance performance is required

core_unit_functions.strong_acid_cation_exchange: exchange radium ions with sodium or other regenerant ions on resin to reduce combined radium in groundwater
core_unit_functions.post_filtration: capture any resin fines and provide final particulate polishing
core_unit_functions.disinfection: maintain microbiological protection in distributed drinking water

applicability_rationale: cation exchange is a widely recognized and proven approach for radium removal from relatively clear groundwater
the source water has low turbidity and low iron and manganese, which supports reliable resin performance
moderate hardness and dissolved solids are compatible with a conventional ion exchange design with proper monitoring
the utility preference for established technology strongly favors this route over more energy-intensive alternatives
potential_risks: regenerant brine will contain concentrated radioactive waste and requires compliant handling
hardness and competing cations can shorten run length and increase regeneration frequency
resin fouling or channeling can reduce removal reliability if pretreatment and maintenance are inadequate

core_unit_functions.lime_softening: precipitate hardness and coprecipitate radium with calcium and magnesium solids
core_unit_functions.solids_separation: remove precipitated solids containing associated radium
core_unit_functions.recarbonation_or_stabilization: adjust finished water chemistry and control scaling or excessive pH
core_unit_functions.disinfection: ensure microbiological safety in the treated supply

applicability_rationale: lime softening is an established radium control method documented for groundwater with appreciable hardness
the raw water hardness and alkalinity make softening technically suitable
this option can provide simultaneous hardness reduction as a co-benefit
it is proven technology but generally more infrastructure-intensive than ion exchange for a small system
potential_risks: radioactive sludge generation may complicate residuals handling
chemical dose control and stabilization are needed to avoid finished water instability
greater footprint and operator attention are required than for packaged exchange systems

core_unit_functions.greensand_or_manganese_dioxide_media_sof: provide media-based removal support where radium reduction occurs through adsorption or association under selected water chemistries, often alongside hardness or metal control strategies
core_unit_functions.filtration: retain media fines and any detached solids
core_unit_functions.disinfection: maintain safe distribution system water quality

applicability_rationale: media-based approaches may be considered when utilities prefer vessel systems and want alternatives to brine-regenerated ion exchange
the groundwater is relatively clean and low in suspended solids, which is favorable for pressurized media operation
this is less universally preferred for radium than ion exchange or lime softening, so it ranks lower despite possible practicality in selected installations
potential_risks: performance for radium can be more site-specific and may require piloting
media exhaustion or ineffective regeneration can lead to breakthrough
treatment claims should be validated with sustained compliance monitoring rather than assumed from vendor literature alone

core_unit_functions.reverse_osmosis: reject dissolved radium and other ions through high-pressure membrane separation
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust corrosivity and remineralize or rebalance product water as needed

applicability_rationale: reverse osmosis is technically capable of removing radium effectively
it may be attractive when broader salinity or multiple dissolved contaminant reduction is also required
for this case, the treatment need is mainly a single radium issue, so the membrane route is not the most proportionate conventional choice
potential_risks: concentrate management creates a radioactive residual stream that may be difficult and costly to dispose
higher energy demand and membrane maintenance exceed the stated preference for proven moderate-complexity treatment
pretreatment may be needed to control scaling in a hard groundwater

core_unit_functions.electrodialysis_reversal: separate dissolved ionic species including radium-associated ions using electrically driven membranes
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust finished water chemistry for distribution compatibility

applicability_rationale: electrodialysis reversal can remove dissolved ions and has been used for selected groundwater desalting applications
it is not usually the first conventional choice for a straightforward radium compliance problem in a small groundwater system
the utility preference for established simple radium-specific treatment makes this a lower-ranked alternative
potential_risks: concentrate disposal remains a major issue for radioactive contaminants
system complexity and specialized operation exceed what is needed for a single-contaminant groundwater case
performance and economics are less attractive than ion exchange under the given constraints

preferred_rank_pattern: cation_exchange should rank first for clear groundwater radium removal under proven technology preference
lime_softening should rank near the top as an established alternative, especially with moderately hard groundwater
high-energy membrane options should not rank first for this single-contaminant case

must_reflect: moderate cost and operational expectations for a small municipal groundwater utility
avoidance of unnecessarily complex high-energy treatment when conventional methods are available

must_contain: why cation exchange is well suited to radium in relatively clear groundwater
why lime softening is a recognized conventional alternative for radium removal
why reverse osmosis and electrodialysis reversal rank lower under this scenario

should_mention: effects of competing cations and hardness on ion exchange performance
need for compliance monitoring to verify sustained radium removal
